items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
Once again I’m tormented
by vague misgivings...
Shpak has a tape recorder,
the ambassador has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Partly successful: the mock-heroic phrasing is preserved via “tormented by vague misgivings,” which keeps the comic high-register self-dramatization. However, the second clause is an accuracy problem per the reference note: «у Шпака — …, у посла — …» here points to the parallel losses/association (each had/has the relevant item in the theft chain), not simple present possession. “Shpak has… the ambassador has…” reads as current ownership and weakens the detective-like inference and the callback logic. A better rendering would avoid “has” (e.g., “Shpak—tape recorder; the ambassador—medallion.” / “Shpak’s tape recorder, the ambassador’s medallion.”).

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Very nice, Tsar!
Very nice—hello, Tsar!

Tsar, very nice!
Hello, Tsar—very nice!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The translation captures the core comic mechanism: repetitive, banal politeness (“Very nice… hello… very nice”) that clashes with the historical setting and Bunsha’s drunken impersonation. However, it drops the self-introduction nuance (“I’m the Tsar”) that in the reference is central to the joke: Bunsha keeps re-presenting himself as “the Tsar” like a Soviet formal greeting ritual. Here he addresses the other person as “Tsar,” which shifts the pragmatic function from self-presentation to deferential address and blunts the era-clash. Still readable and funny-ish, but the key comedic vector is weakened.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Essentially perfect: preserves the authoritarian, performative “royal” demand and the comic escalation. Slightly clipped grammar (“I demand the banquet continue”) is acceptable subtitle-style and keeps punchiness.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I’m in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Perfect (functionally identical). Keeps the archaic/high-register melancholy and the comic mismatch with the mundane setting. Contraction “I’m” is a tiny modernization but doesn’t harm the effect in subtitles.

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Meaning is conveyed (he believes he’s been sealed in by demons), but the English is awkward and slightly unclear: “Bricked up!” reads like a fragment and can evoke unrelated slang; it also loses the passive/subject relation emphasized in the reference (demons as the agents who walled him in). The comedic melodrama works better with an explicit “They’ve walled me in…” structure. Current version risks momentary confusion and weakens the mock-tragic tone.

items.translation: See what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Good and on-point: preserves the religious exclamation and the causal misattribution (miracle vs. elevator mechanics). “See what … can do” is slightly more colloquial than “Behold the power,” but still fits and reads naturally in subtitles.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Conveys the basic directive to dance, but loses the specific performative/MC-like announcement quality that makes it funnier in context (a bureaucrat-turned-tsar opening the dance floor with a grand, public formula). “Everybody dance!” is understandable yet flatter and less ‘announcer’/ceremonial. A closer comedic equivalent would be “Everyone dance!” / “All of you—dance!” / “Everybody’s dancing!” or an emcee line like “All right—everyone on the floor!”

items.translation: Well, this is a lucky visit!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Works well: keeps the breezy self-satisfaction and the sense of opportunistic luck. “Lucky visit” is slightly more formal than the jaunty criminal vibe of the original, but still lands the beat and is idiomatic.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
and yes! Imported caviar—
eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Major creative/clarity miss: the reference explicitly notes not to call the eggplant spread “caviar,” because the joke hinges on the Soviet “eggplant caviar” being a cheap spread presented as exotic; English viewers will likely parse “imported caviar—eggplant” as literal eggplant-flavored caviar or a category error, not as a satirical inversion of scarcity/status. Using “overseas spread—eggplant” (or “eggplant spread”) preserves the gag and avoids confusion. Current version risks losing the cultural punchline.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything, everything I earned
by backbreaking labor,

is gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
three suede jackets.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The list is mostly accurate and keeps the escalating inventory-comedy, but it misses a key micro-beat: in the original he says “куртка… замшевая… три” (singular “jacket” then the delayed “three”), which is funny because he momentarily fumbles the count and then corrects/lands on “three.” The translation normalizes it to “three suede jackets,” removing the stuttered reveal and the comic rhythm. Also, “domestic” is attached to cigarette cases in the reference as a self-important detail; here it’s fine, but the ellipsis/hesitation is lost. This is a creative timing loss rather than a factual error.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Perfect: preserves the calm, rhythmic, resigned escalation and the darkly comic shared “we’re all going to the psych ward” acceptance. Punctuation/exclamation marks are a minor tonal shift toward more energy, but the comedic effect remains intact.

summary.interpretation: 2.91 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.58 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
